old_claim.note: Fire Control is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim_absent: No fire-control dependency remains because the identified Schnidejoch method uses an aqueous extract of plant material and does not require smoke tanning or heating.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract identifies aqueous extracts of diverse plant material as the tanning agent; Introduction lists smoke tanning only as a different possible method.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Chemical biomarkers support a water-based plant treatment for these artifacts rather than a smoke-based tanning route.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Fire may support other tanning traditions, but it is not a required operation for the specifically evidenced aqueous process.

ontology_before: All tanning methods inherited fire because smoke is one possible hide treatment.
ontology_after: The Schnidejoch plant-extract method is represented without an unrelated smoke-processing edge.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: leather_tanning no longer directly depends on fire_control.
would_reject_if: Analysis established that heating or smoke was necessary to prepare the Schnidejoch tanning extract.
short_reason: The evidenced tanning agent is aqueous plant extract, not smoke.
